标题: What xAI and OpenAI Should Buy Next
内容:
Welcome, Weekenders!
In this newsletter:
•
The Big Read:
The
rise of SemiAnalysis’ Dylan Patel
, Silicon Valley’s favorite AI commentator
•
Plus, Recommendations—our weekly pop culture picks:
“
The Obit Project
,” “
Disneyland and the Rise of Automation
” and “
Unfamiliar
”
Recently, a close friend
of mine introduced me to another of his friends, a tech investment banker, and now this banker and I have become friendly, too. I’ve enjoyed learning a bit about his life. We’d not met before because he’s not around very much, with AI and tech and FOMO what they are these days. He lives right by one of the nicest beaches in California and has bought himself an expensive car. But he seldom seems to spend much time enjoying his seaside abode, and he’s burning the gas in his G-Wagon mostly to get back to the airport. He travels often for work, sometimes several times a week.
By his telling of it, investment banking sounds like an arduous, go-go-go profession. I don’t buy it, though, because it seems simple enough to me, and as it happens, over the past few days I’ve come up with a bunch of very plausible deals I think should happen.
Here’s the most straightforward one: xAI should buy Cursor. The two already have a blossoming relationship, with xAI agreeing
to sell computing capacity
to Cursor. That makes sense. The compute crunch is real. The fine folks at SemiAnalysis, the AI research outfit profiled
in our latest Big Read
,
recently likened
acquiring AI processing power to “trying to book airplane tickets on the last flight out.”
Joining up with xAI would give Cursor access to xAI’s “stockpile” of compute, as
Business Insider termed it
. (Imagine having a stockpile of compute! That’d be like laying out a smorgasbord in front of Tom Hanks in “Castaway.”) xAI, meanwhile, would get a leg up in the AI coding wars and gain its first real access to the enterprise market.
My second idea is slightly less plausible but still very fun to consider. I think Snap CEO Evan Spiegel should sell the company to OpenAI. Snap
is slashing its staff
in yet another attempt to engineer a turnaround, and it won’t work. None of Spiegel’s other turnaround efforts have. He should accept the inevitable and take the years he’s spent focusing on consumer hardware to a place that really, really wants to do consumer hardware. An added incentive: Whatever he came up with at OpenAI would have a much better chance of torpedoing Meta Platforms’ efforts to produce similar technology. And surely he
would still like
to take Zuck down a peg or ten.
At Snap, Spiegel has total power over the stock thanks to his supervoting shares. He’s the only one who can make the decision on a sale, and he has seemed resistant to the idea even as the stock has dropped and dropped and dropped. (A CEO with less control of the stock would’ve been ousted long ago.) But I think OpenAI’s recent acquisition of TBPN is real proof that it can
win over
founders who wouldn’t sell to anyone else.
Finally, let’s ponder what will happen to poor ol’ Vox Media, which will
reportedly
begin selling itself piecemeal. Vox owns more than 10 media brands, including New York Magazine, itself a collection of brands. If someone hasn’t already called Jay Penske, who owns a portion of the Vox parent company, they should—and they should talk him into buying Vulture, New York’s beloved outlet devoted to entertainment and Hollywood. Puck News
says
Karli Kloss has been interested in possibly buying The Cut, another beloved New York sub-brand. I find her interest intriguing: A couple years ago, I
predicted
that Vox Media would need to do a sale like this one and would sell New York Magazine to her husband, Josh Kushner. Clearly, my crystal ball tuned into the wrong corner of the Kushner-Kloss living room.
Vox also owns The Verge, the tech news site. I’m sure the same set of people who keep loudly talking about wanting to buy Wired might be interested in The Verge. They’d be buying it to profoundly change it, and I wonder if Vox CEO Jim Bankoff would want to be the one to push those brands into their hands. I also wonder if much of the Vox brands would get sold off to owners who seem to apparate out of nowhere, as Jeff Lawson did when
he bought
The Onion.
I’m gonna stop here and go try to track down that banker I mentioned. I expect a fat finder’s fee.
—Abram Brown (
abe@theinformation.com
) .
Weekend’s Latest Stories
The Big Read
The Rise of SemiAnalysis’ Dylan Patel, Silicon Valley’s Favorite AI Commentator
The 29-year-old
has adeptly navigated
the blurred lines between media, tech and investing, turning himself into a hybrid of all three.
Listening:
“
The Obit Project
”
Ostensibly, an obituary is meant to speak about a single person and their past. Of course, the finest obituaries have greater depth, offering a picture of the broader circumstances around whoever they’re trying to memorialize. “The Obit Project,” a podcast co-led by Radiolab creator Jad Abumrad, definitely falls into that latter category. Its series of obituaries are marvelous little earworms, full of charm, warmth and depth, each about a death in Montana. It’s “This American Life” trussed up in bear grass: The obits evoke a specific, singular place, but they have a universal resonance, documenting lives that take the shape of familiar archetypes: a restless, small-town salesman, an artist fleeing the city for love and nature, an immigrant who dies tragically far from her home. Abumrad and his team, which includes a former colleague from WNYC, Jule Banville, and her University of Montana journalism students, approach the task with pathos and playfulness. That immigrant I mentioned was in fact Old Pitt, a circus elephant struck dead by lightning in 1943.
—Abram Brown
Reading:
“
Disneyland and the Rise of Automation
” by Rolan Betancourt
Disneyland may seem like a place conjured from fairy dust, but that’s a misconception author Roland Betancourt, a University of California, Irvine, professor with a varied expertise in pop culture, art and the Byzantine Empire, is eager to dispel. He views it as a modern technological marvel, and his thorough research account shows how Walt Disney melded innovations from people like Henry Ford into the theme park to create the seamless dream world he wanted.
“The Evil Witch popping out of a corner in the Snow White ride is not merely a scare tactic, but an audio-visual stimulus triggered by a limit switch as your vehicle approaches a designated location,” writes Betancourt. “Every Disneyland ride is a carefully camouflaged assembly line where you are its signature product.”
—A.B.
Watching:
“
Unfamiliar
”
For those people missing “The Americans,” “Unfamiliar,” a new German spy drama available on Netflix, is a worthy replacement. Set in Berlin in present day, the six-episode show centers on husband-and-wife undercover spies who may or may not have retired—their exact status is a little fuzzy—but who get pulled back into action when the legacy of a 16-year-old operation resurfaces. If that sounds a little cliched, so be it. But the slick pacing and great performances, particularly from leads Susanne Wolf and Felix Kramer, make it highly enjoyable.
—Martin Peers

标题: Anthropic’s CFO Wields Power Behind the Scenes
内容:
These days, Anthropic is a crowded place: a startup with its own in-house philosopher, researchers devoted to preventing Armageddon and a CEO willing to go toe-to-toe with the Pentagon. And then there’s Chief Financial Officer Krishna Rao, who has emerged as a key force within the company while staying far out of the spotlight.
When Rao first arrived in 2024, he began pushing Anthropic, which had an agreement with Google for cloud computing, to strike deals with multiple chip providers and cloud partners. One of the people he discussed the plan with was Byron Deeter, a partner at Bessemer Venture Partners, an Anthropic investor. Deeter credits Rao with the insight that Anthropic could be growing even faster with more partners.
Anthropic now has deals with all three major cloud providers—Google, Amazon and Microsoft—and runs its models on chips from Nvidia, Google and Amazon. And it was quicker to diversify than its chief rival, OpenAI, which has now followed its path.
More recently, Rao was the main driver at Anthropic in
crafting an important joint venture
with Blackstone and other private equity firms, an effort that will allow Anthropic to sell its AI to those firms’ portfolio companies, according to three people involved in the project. Just a few weeks ago, Anthropic dispatched Rao to a gathering at the beachfront Bahamas estate owned by Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon, where Rao was a hit among public and private market investors, as well as a few top Goldman bankers, said multiple attendees.
“There are CFOs who came up through accounting and produce the right numbers but aren’t really commercially sophisticated. Krishna is not like that,” said Martin Brand, who worked with Rao when he was at Blackstone. (Brand currently runs the firm’s flagship private equity fund.) “He has a real sense of what’s important, what matters to a business and what matters to the person he’s talking to.”
As competition around AI continues to magnify, Rao finds himself in a high-pressure situation and a role far more complex than the one usually handed to a startup’s CFO. There are the ordinary mergers and acquisitions tasks, for which Rao, age 43, has recruited a small army of ex-bankers and investors, who are scouting for
startup acquisitions
in
technology
,
healthcare
and life sciences.
More importantly, he’s contending with the enormous capital demands of developing AI, which requires overcoming a number of scarcity and supply issues—most notably, the growing demand for chip clusters from cloud providers like Amazon and Google. Anthropic needs those clusters to train its AI models.
“He’s technically very deep in the business,” said Chris Emanuel of Singapore sovereign wealth fund GIC, which co-led the company’s latest $30 billion fundraise.
To pay for everything the company needs, Rao has steered Anthropic through multiple funding rounds to raise $60 billion since he joined, with its valuation rising to $380 billion in January. As he raised this “quantum of capital,” Rao has done a “phenomenal job of communicating the story with transparency to investors,” said Matt Jacobson, a partner at Iconiq, the venture firm that co-led the company’s fundraising at a $183 billion valuation last September.
Anthropic, however, has no plans to raise any more money at the moment, said two people close to the company. If the company were to raise, it likely won’t happen until after its May board meeting, where the company plans to discuss all of its financing options, said a person with knowledge of its plans. Rao and his team have received offers to invest in the company at an $800 billion valuation—and some investors think it could even fetch $1 trillion, according to people familiar with its thinking.
Rao must also keep Anthropic on track for an initial public offering, which
could come as early as this fall
, though it hasn’t made its plans public. While Rao played a major role in Airbnb’s 2020 public debut, Anthropic’s listing would be a major test of his abilities, a fact not lost on Anthropic’s investors. “He’s certainly super smart and seems like a good guy. He’s also very green,” said a person who has been involved in Anthropic’s fundraising. “He hasn’t operated at this scale before.”
Rao is a stark contrast to his peer at OpenAI:
Sarah Friar
, a longtime Silicon Valley fixture, who was previously CFO at Square and CEO of Nextdoor and is a director at Walmart. As OpenAI prepares for its listing, Friar has cultivated a high public profile, making herself readily available to the media. Rao, by contrast, dislikes any sort of spotlight. He may very well be the only AI executive of his stature
not
to have appeared on a tech podcast.
Through a spokesperson, Rao declined to be interviewed for this story.
The competition between Rao’s and Friar’s companies is fierce: OpenAI and Anthropic are battling for the same chips, investors, employees and customers. Anthropic’s revenue has been a rocket ship lately, and its two recent megahit products, Claude Code and Cowork, drove annualized revenue
past $30 billion earlier this month
—more than three times what the figure was at year end and possibly a hair past OpenAI’s figure.
But the company’s growth has started to raise questions—and has invited
swipes from rival OpenAI
—about whether Anthropic has secured
enough of the AI servers it needs
.
CEO Dario Amodei
has noted
that accurately estimating the massive computing needs the company will need years into the future will comprise the difference between success and bankruptcy. A lot of the responsibility for getting that calculation right lies with Rao.
Rao’s mentor, Laurence Tosi, who worked with Rao at Blackstone and then at Airbnb, thinks Rao can handle it because he has been steeped in the concept of “disciplined growth” over his career.
“He’s not going to lose money on hope or raise it at stupid valuations,” he said.
For guidance through this high-wire act, Rao will lean on what he’s learned from a fast-tracked career across private equity that propelled him into the Silicon Valley elite.
Born in Minneapolis and raised in both Southern California and Minnesota, Rao graduated from Harvard University in 2005 with a summa cum laude degree in economics and then spent three years as a Bain & Co. consultant. He next got a Yale Law School degree and earned a coveted internship for the Supreme Court. But rather than pursue a career as an attorney, he got a job at Blackstone.
Brand, one of the Blackstone executives who interviewed him at the time, recalls thinking before he met Rao that he might not make the cut. Unlike many other candidates, he didn’t have any investment banking experience or previous time in private equity. But after a round of brain-teaser questions, Brand was convinced, and the firm hired Rao as a senior associate.
“I thought he was smart,” Brand recalled. “Then the other thing about him was he was engaging: He had a pretty dry sense of humor—somebody you’d like to hang out with.”
For all Rao’s prowess analyzing companies’ finances, where he shone the most was negotiating in difficult situations. Brand recalled a particularly challenging one at a company Blackstone took private. Rao, who represented Blackstone on the board, wrestled with slow-to-change executives. “He was very competent, very calm no matter what happened,” said Brand.
By 2015, Rao had eyes on the West Coast, and after interviewing at Uber and Airbnb, he took a job at Airbnb. There he reunited with Tosi, who had left a job as Blackstone’s CFO to do the same thing for Airbnb. Rao started off in financial planning and analysis at Airbnb and quickly rose to lead the startup’s corporate and business development, pushing it to invest in Resy, a restaurant app, and buy HotelTonight for $400 million.
By early 2020, the company was headed for a public listing. But then the pandemic hit, sending its business plummeting.
As losses mounted
, Airbnb scrapped its listing plans and focused on weathering the crisis. To do so, it needed cash. Rao ran the negotiations that got Airbnb the money it needed.
On April 6, Airbnb said it was getting $1 billion in emergency funding from Sixth Street and Silver Lake. Within a week, the company had lined up an additional $1 billion in debt from an investor group including BlackRock, Fidelity and T. Rowe Price.
By summer, Airbnb’s business started to rebound, along with its plans to go public. In early December that year, it raised more than $3.5 billion at a $47 billion valuation in an IPO, surging to a market cap over $100 billion at the end of its first trading day.
“He was really the guy on the business side, running their IPO,” said a lawyer who worked with Rao during Airbnb’s IPO. “I thought he did a great job coordinating it all, interfacing with the banks and the executives at Airbnb.”
After his time at Airbnb, Rao made a number of quick career moves—first becoming CFO at Cedar, a healthcare payments startup, and then CFO of the commerce division at Fanatics, a sports-merchandise startup.
The divisional CFO job at Fanatics was a step back for Rao in the corporate hierarchy, but he didn’t stay in it for very long. A year later, he was considering joining Anthropic as CFO.
When Anthropic began to seriously consider Rao, Anthropic co-founder Daniela Amodei called one of his references, Tosi, who sang Rao’s praises as loudly as he could. Rao got the job shortly afterward.
“They wanted someone who understood financing, risk, investors and public markets,” Tosi said. “They didn’t want a Valley CFO who grew up as a controller at a software company.”
Before Rao’s arrival, Anthropic co-founder Tom Brown, previously at OpenAI and Google DeepMind, was leading most of the company’s business-related functions, said a person close to the company. But he needed help from a finance executive with a background in fundraising.
When Rao joined in March 2024, he inherited a business valued at around $15 billion and generating in the low hundreds of millions in annualized revenue. In some ways, though, it was still run like a research lab.
Anthropic’s board meetings mostly focused on tech, policy and AI safety. Rao introduced a section to look more explicitly at the company’s business and financial performance, including enterprise software metrics and key leading indicators of growth, said one of the people.
To get up to speed, Rao immediately dug into the company’s gross margins and evaluated how to better manage compute for running and training Anthropic’s models. The changes he made had a significant impact. Anthropic jumped from 94% negative gross margins in 2024 to 40% positive gross margins the next year. That improvement coincided with annualized revenue increasing to $9 billion from under $1 billion over the same time period. OpenAI ended 2025 with a 33% gross margin and $21 billion in annualized revenue.
Rao assembled a team to help him make acquisitions, last May hiring Andrew Zloto, a former partner at SoftBank’s Vision Fund who had worked with Rao at Airbnb. (Some bankers and investors described Zloto as “crucial” to Rao and said he has taken over some of his boss’s responsibilities, including certain meetings with investors and investment bankers.)
At the same time, Anthropic hasn’t been in a hurry to make splashy acquisitions, like OpenAI’s $5 billion all-stock deal for Io, the hardware startup co-founded by former Apple design chief Jony Ive. Rao and his lieutenants have told bankers they prefer writing checks smaller than $500 million for companies with promising technologies or teams of talented researchers and engineers.
Still, bankers and investors have flooded Anthropic with emails and phone calls pitching it on deals. The corporate development team has turned to using Claude to sift through all the incoming communication and analyze the companies, according to people who have spoken with its executives.
Anthropic has since lured away other seasoned corporate development executives from public tech companies. In recent months, it’s hired Vishal Kumar Gupta, a vice president at ServiceNow, who used to lead ServiceNow’s global corporate development, and Matt Devine, who joined from Meta Platforms and before that Google.
Rao has run a tight ship on Anthropic’s fundraising, and that jives with how Jonathan Mildenhall, a former chief marketing officer at Airbnb and a Fanatics board member, described him. “He controls things very, very tightly,” said Mildenhall.
Investors have fought to get into the startup’s fundraising rounds, and some have fretted over the strict terms Rao has enforced. When Anthropic was raising its $13 billion round announced in September, for instance, it pushed to have investors write checks for the hundreds of millions of dollars—amounting to half an entire raise for some venture fund, according to four people familiar with the fundraise. (That’s on par with OpenAI, which also required investment minimums of its investors, according to a person with knowledge of OpenAI’s fundraising.)
Even the investors that managed to pool together enough money to invest in the company were disappointed when they could not receive the stake that they wanted, given there was so much demand to invest in the company.
But fundraising is just half the battle for CFOs in the age of AI. Rao also played a role in negotiating and managing compute agreements to ensure Anthropic had enough servers to run and train its AI models.
These included two late last year to spend
$30 billion on Microsoft cloud servers
powered by Nvidia chips and another to use
up to 1 million of Google’s tensor processing units
. Then earlier this month announced a new deal with
Broadcom and Google
to acquire multiple gigawatts of data center power. These added to its multi-faceted partnership with Amazon Web Services, whose vice president of corporate development, Ankush Prakash, said of Rao: “we look forward to continuing to work with [him] for years to come.”
Given all the pressure, Bessemer’s Deeter thinks Rao has managed it all pretty well. “This has been the most in-demand company for investment,” he said. “And he’s managed that dynamic really well.…He does what he says and he doesn’t overpromise.”
